title: The Side-Channel Where Tributaries Remember

features.name: the confluence of lost names
features.desc-on-touch: Three tributaries meet here, each carrying water from a different town. The water remembers the names of places it left behind—you hear them in the current if you listen without thinking.

features.name: the silted record-keeper
features.desc-on-touch: The riverbed here is layered with fine silt from three different geological periods. Each layer remembers what the land looked like when it washed down. The archive reads by dust.

features.name: the bend where water becomes archive
features.desc-on-touch: The current has slowed here, dropping its freight of history. A sandbar accumulates documents: stone, bone, metal, wood—all things the smaller rivers wanted to keep.

features.name: the reeds that speak in names
features.desc-on-touch: The reeds here vibrate at frequencies that match the names of drowned towns. They hum the places the water came from, storing the names in sound.

features.name: the branch-ladder that holds time
features.desc-on-touch: Fallen trees have caught against the banks, creating natural ladders between water levels. Each rung marks where the flood was that year. The tree reads its own history vertically.

features.name: the listening post at the eddy
features.desc-on-touch: A still point in the current where the water circles endlessly. This is where the channel keeps its secrets—the archive stores only that which refuses to flow downstream.

inscription: the side channel is where the river keeps the things it chose not to carry all the way. Here the forgotten tributaries teach the current to remember

file.name: TributaryGauges_ThreeDecadesOfWater.mp3
file.note: A field recording of three different tributary-voices recorded simultaneously—sometimes in harmony, sometimes in argument, always comparing the water they carry from homes they refuse to forget.

exits.label: back to where the ruins remember the water
downstream where smaller channels join the main argument
along the channel toward a tributary that no longer flows
